title: Title

onScreen.heading: How to Handle Large Datasets on the Web
onScreen.subtitle: When local-first meets real-world scale

talkingPoints: Welcome, today I want to talk about a problem that is massively under-discussed in the local-first community.
We have incredible resources for CRDTs, sync, conflict resolution. Nobody talks about what happens when all that data lands in the browser.
I hit this wall. Here's what I learned.

title: How Does a Local-First App Work?

onScreen.heading: How Does a Local-First App Work?
onScreen.flow: FETCH → PERSIST → LOAD → RENDER (Cloud → Database → RAM Chip → Browser)
onScreen.quote: This flow works beautifully. At small scale.

talkingPoints: How does a local-first app work? Let's build it step by step.
First, fetch from the server — once, or incrementally. Persist to the user's device — that's what makes it local-first.
On open, load from disk into memory — JS objects in the heap. Then hydrate the UI — DOM elements render.
This flow works beautifully. At small scale.

title: How I Got Here

onScreen.heading: How I Got Here
steps: 02 — Custom CRDT sync engine, no third-party libs
03 — Started building the web version with IDB
04 — Tested with real data. App collapsed at 10K tasks.

talkingPoints: I'm Riken, founding engineer at Tasks. 700K+ downloads, 4.8 stars. Built our sync engine from scratch — custom CRDT, no third-party libs.
Started building the web version with IDB.
Tested with real data. App collapsed at 10K tasks.

title: Then We Hit 10,000 Tasks

onScreen.heading: Then We Hit 10,000 Tasks
onScreen.quote: V0 looked great. Then real users showed up.
onScreen.visual: Three grids side by side: 100 (smooth, checkmark) → 10K (laggy, pulsing, warning) → 100K (frozen, unresponsive, explosion)
onScreen.footer: Same app. Same code. Just more data.

talkingPoints: V0 looked great. Then real data showed up.
At 100 tasks — smooth. At 10K — laggy, things start pulsing. At 100K — frozen. Page unresponsive.
Same app. Same code. Just more data. Local-first does not mean all data in memory. This is a problem that grows with your users' success.

title: 4 Walls I Hit

onScreen.heading: 4 Walls I Hit
onScreen.subtitle: Each one nearly killed the web version.
problems: 01 Memory — Can't keep everything in the JS heap
04 Storage Speed — Random access is too slow

talkingPoints: Building a local-first app at scale means solving all four of these.
Memory — you can't load everything. DOM — 10K nodes kills the browser. Updates — one change re-renders the whole board. Storage speed — random access is too slow.
Each one nearly killed the web version.

title: Problem 1: Memory

onScreen.label: Problem 1
onScreen.heading: We Can't Keep Everything in Memory
onScreen.detail: Observable fields, proxy wrappers, junction maps

talkingPoints: Every entity has real overhead. If you use MobX or any reactive framework — observable fields, proxy wrappers, junction maps — each entity costs ~14KB.
25K tasks × 14KB = 350MB. Just for task objects. Before properties, comments, or rendering.
Chrome tabs crash around 1-2GB. One large project eats 350MB of that budget.

title: Problem 2: DOM Rendering

onScreen.label: Problem 2
onScreen.heading: DOM Rendering is Expensive
onScreen.visual: Three growing grids: 100 (white, checkmark) → 1,400 (gray, warning) → 10K+ (dark, explosion)
bullets: ✕ Style recalculation across the entire tree on every change
✕ Layout reflows recompute positions for every element
💥 Adding 1 node to 10K? Browser recomputes everything.

talkingPoints: More nodes = exponentially more work. Style recalculation scales with DOM size. Layout reflows recompute positions for the entire tree.
Lighthouse warns at 800 nodes, errors at 1,400. At 10K+ — every interaction lags.
Adding ONE node to a 10K tree triggers a recalculation of the entire tree.

title: Problem 3: Mutation Handling

onScreen.label: Problem 3
onScreen.heading: Mutation Handling
onScreen.subtitle: Databases like DexieDB give you table-level hooks. When one task changes, the entire table re-queries.
onScreen.visual: 5×4 grid of blocks all blinking — ONE TASK CHANGES → ENTIRE TABLE RE-RENDERS
onScreen.detail: Every component re-evaluates. Every card re-renders.
onScreen.footer: No onChange, no triggers. How do we make updates surgical?

talkingPoints: Databases like DexieDB give you table-level hooks only. When one task changes, the entire table re-queries. All blocks re-render.
O(n) re-evaluation — even though only one thing changed.
IDB gives us no onChange, no triggers. We have to build this ourselves.

title: What Everyone Tries

onScreen.heading: What Everyone Tries
onScreen.subtitle: Windowing solves most of it. But none avoid re-querying on every update.

approaches.name: Pagination
approaches.desc: Split into pages, load one at a time

approaches.name: Virtualization
approaches.desc: Recycle DOM nodes, all data in heap

onScreen.punchline: Updates — always off. Every change re-queries the entire dataset.
onScreen.footer: You can pick Memory + DOM. But who avoids the re-renders?

talkingPoints: So you Google it. Pagination — solves memory, breaks UX. Virtualization — solves DOM, everything still in memory. Windowing — gets the closest.
Solves memory, DOM, scoping. But none of them avoid re-querying on every update.
You can flip two switches but never all three.

title: Windowing Gets Closest

onScreen.heading: Windowing Gets Closest
onScreen.visual: Tall list: 5 visible items (white), 2 buffer (gray), rest dashed/empty
bullets: Both DOM and data are windowed. Only ~30 items in memory.
⚠ Needs fast random access from storage
onScreen.footer: So: windowing is the shape of the answer. But I need faster storage + reactivity.

talkingPoints: Windowing manages both DOM and data. Only ~30 items in memory and DOM. Buffer zone preloads nearby items.
Solves memory, DOM, scoping. But it needs fast random access reads — individual items on scroll.
And no built-in mutation tracking. Closest to what we need, but not the full answer.

title: Each Storage Has a Strength

onScreen.heading: Each Storage Has a Strength
onScreen.meta: 100K tasks · real queries · median of 3 runs

randomAccess.note: most important for our use case

onScreen.punchline: Neither is better. What if we use both?

talkingPoints: I benchmarked IDB against SQLite WASM on OPFS. 100K tasks, real queries.
Random access — the one windowing needs — OPFS is 4.4x faster. 8.5ms vs 37.6ms.
Sequential reads — IDB wins by 1.9x. Writes — OPFS 2.4x faster.
Neither is strictly better. Each has a strength. What if we use both?

title: What If We Only Load What's Visible?

onScreen.heading: What If We Only Load What's Visible?
onScreen.subtitle: A viewport-aware reactive rendering engine.
onScreen.architecture: UI → EntityStore (indexes + hydrated visible) → Database → Disk
onScreen.visual: 16 rows: 6 visible (glowing white, 'hydrated'), 2 buffer (gray), rest ('index only'). Floating particles rising from disk.
onScreen.footer: Scroll in → hydrate from disk. Scroll out → unload, keep index. Memory stays ~7MB.
onScreen.note: zero third-party deps except MobX for observability

talkingPoints: A viewport-aware, reactive rendering engine. Only loads what users see.
Architecture: UI at the top, EntityStore in the middle — holds indexes plus hydrated visible entities — Database and Disk below.
Look at the right side — items in the dataset, only the visible ones are hydrated. The rest are lightweight indexes.
Scroll in → hydrate. Scroll out → unload. Memory stays flat.

title: How EntityStore Works

onScreen.heading: How EntityStore Works
onScreen.subtitle: Register a query once. EntityStore keeps it alive — indexes update without re-rendering.

steps.title: EntityStore returns indexes
steps.detail: Lightweight pointers to every matching entity. Not full objects.

steps.title: Query becomes observable
steps.detail: Task added, moved, or deleted? Indexes auto-update. No re-query.

steps.title: Minimal re-renders
steps.detail: Only the affected visible entity re-renders. Everything else stays untouched.

onScreen.visual: Live kanban board (To Do / Active / Done) with green pulsing matched cards. '✓ always in sync / query never re-runs'
onScreen.punchline: Query once → observe forever. Minimal re-renders. No recomputation.

talkingPoints: Here's how the observable query system works.
Step one: you register your query with EntityStore.
Step two: it returns indexes — lightweight pointers to every matching entity.
Step three: that query becomes observable. Task added, moved, deleted? The indexes auto-update. No re-query.
Step four: minimal re-renders — only the affected visible entity re-renders. Everything else stays untouched.
Query once, observe forever.

title: How We Do This: MobX

onScreen.heading: How We Do This: MobX
onScreen.subtitle: EntityStore uses MobX under the hood. Each entity is independently observable.

entityCascade.label: A task is never just a task:
entityCascade.detail: 1 task = ~9 entities. MobX makes each one independently observable.

onScreen.credit: Inspired by Linear's approach to per-entity observability at scale.

talkingPoints: EntityStore uses MobX under the hood. Each entity is independently observable.
Without MobX — left side — entire list re-queries and diffs. O(n).
With MobX — right side — only the changed card re-renders. O(1). Surgical.
And remember: a task is never just a task. Properties, tags, comments, assets. 1 task = ~9 entities. MobX makes each one independently observable.
Credit to the Linear team for pioneering this pattern.

title: Why Indexes Instead of Full Entities?

onScreen.heading: Why Indexes Instead of Full Entities?
onScreen.barGraph: Full Entity bar (100%) vs Index Only bar (1.4%) — visual memory comparison

talkingPoints: Full entities: each carries observable fields, proxy wrappers, junction maps. ~14KB each. At 25K — 350MB. Tab crashes.
Indexes: four fields. Plain JS objects. ~200 bytes. At 25K — 5MB.
Same 25K tasks. 70x less memory. Look at the bar graph — the difference is staggering.

title: The Hybrid Model

onScreen.heading: The Hybrid Model
onScreen.flow: IDB (indexes, sequential) → 'startup' → EntityStore (25K indexes + ~150 visible) → 'on scroll' → SQLite (full data, WASM + OPFS)

talkingPoints: On startup, load all indexes from IDB. One sequential read — IDB is 1.9x faster here. 25K lightweight objects. Board renders immediately.
As the user scrolls, pull full data from SQLite via OPFS — random access. OPFS is 4.4x faster here.
Card scrolls out → full data freed, index retained.
IDB fires once at startup. SQLite fires continuously as the user interacts. Each storage does what it's best at.

title: The Numbers
talkingPoints: The proof. Before: 350MB. Freezes. After: 7MB. 50x reduction.
250ms to interactive with 25,000 tasks.

title: Memory Stays Flat
talkingPoints: Look at the graph. Naive approach — red line shoots up linearly. 10K to 100K goes from 140MB to 1.4GB.
Two-tier — green line stays flat. ~7MB to ~9MB. About 10% growth.
This works at 5K or 500K.

title: Closing

onScreen.quote: The best sync engine in the world doesn't matter if your app takes 3 seconds to load a task list.
onScreen.message: The unglamorous work is what makes local-first apps feel fast.
onScreen.note: And none of it required a third-party library.

talkingPoints: The best sync engine in the world doesn't matter if your app takes 3 seconds to load a task list.
The unglamorous work is what makes local-first apps feel fast.
And none of it required a third-party library.
Thank you.
